questions.specification: Which process involves material extrusion guided by a 3D CAD model?
Joining techniques include...
What is the primary function of finishing techniques in manufacturing?

options.content: To reduce the material cost of a product
To enhance the surface appearance and increase product longevity
To speed up the production process

questions.specification: Which process enables printing in five different directions?

options.markscheme: 5D printing utilizes a five-axis system, allowing the print head to approach the object from five different directions, which improves strength and reduces the need for support structures.
Dual-extrusion printing involves using two different filaments or colours, but typically operates on a standard three-axis system.
4D printing involves 3D printed objects that change their shape or properties over time in response to external stimuli.
Laminated object manufacturing (LOM) is a rapid prototyping process that involves layering and cutting sheets of material; it does not involve five-axis printing.

questions.specification: What best describes subtractive manufacturing?

options.content: Removing material to shape a product
Cutting a flat sheet into pieces and assembling them to form a shape

questions.specification: What is the purpose of organizing manufacturing techniques into five categories?

options.content: To group methods based on how they shape materials

questions.specification: Joining techniques are a critical consideration in product design, influencing both how a product is manufactured and how it is used throughout its lifecycle.

parts.content: Explain how joining techniques impact product functionality and user experience.
parts.markscheme:
Joining techniques affect both the mechanical performance of a product and the way a user interacts with it. 

*   **Permanent joining (e.g., welding or brazing):** These techniques create high-strength, rigid bonds <marks>1</marks> which ensure the product can withstand heavy loads or stress without mechanical failure, improving functionality <marks>1</marks>.
*   **Temporary joining (e.g., mechanical fasteners like screws or bolts):** These allow for the product to be non-destructively disassembled <marks>1</marks>, which enhances the user experience by facilitating easy maintenance, repair, or the replacement of worn-out parts <marks>1</marks>.
*   **Adhesives and thermal joining:** These methods can eliminate the need for visible external fasteners, reducing the overall weight of the product <marks>1</marks> and creating a cleaner aesthetic, which improves the visual and tactile user experience <marks>1</marks>.

*Award [1] for identifying a relevant joining technique or impact and [1] for a corresponding explanation of its effect on functionality or user experience, up to [6 marks] total.*

questions.specification: Which technique reshapes materials without adding or removing them?

options.markscheme: Forming techniques change the shape of a material through force (such as bending or vacuum forming) without the addition or removal of material.
Cutting is a wasting technique that involves the removal of material to achieve a desired shape.
Casting involves melting a material and pouring it into a mould; while it occupies a shape, 'forming' is the specific term defined by reshaping without gain or loss.
Joining is an additive technique that involves combining two or more parts, thereby adding material.

questions.specification: Which technique involves layer-by-layer fabrication by fusing plastic layers together using heat?

What type of manufacturing technique is most likely being used in this process?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A. Design in theory > A4.1 Manufacturing techniques (HL only) > A4.1.10 Finishing techniques
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A4.1.10 Finishing techniques
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks to compare and contrast three finishing techniques, which is the direct focus of the 'Finishing techniques' leaf topic.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B. Design in practice > B4.1 Production systems (HL only) > B4.1.3 Factors affecting manufacturing technique choice
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B4.1.3 Factors affecting manufacturing technique choice
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Discussing the 'impact on product quality' is a key factor affecting the choice of a specific manufacturing or finishing technique in a production context.

parts.content: Compare and contrast three finishing techniques and their impact on product quality.
parts.markscheme:
- Anodizing creates an integral oxide layer which significantly improves corrosion resistance and appearance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Electroplating increases surface hardness and provides a high-quality aesthetic finish through metal deposition <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Powder coating provides a thick, uniform coverage that is highly durable and resistant to chipping <marks>1 mark</marks>
- All three techniques extend the product's lifespan and reduce the frequency of maintenance required <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improved visual appeal from these finishes increases user satisfaction and perceived product quality <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Each technique contributes to long-term product protection by shielding the substrate from environmental degradation <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each valid point of comparison or contrast, up to [6 max].*

questions.specification: What distinguishes 4D printing from traditional 3D printing?

options.content: Objects change shape in response to stimuli
Objects incorporate embedded sensors and actuators to enable movement

questions.specification: Why is a combination of additive, subtractive, forming, joining, and finishing techniques often necessary in product creation?

options.content: Because each technique has the same purpose
Because different manufacturers prefer different techniques
Because each contributes uniquely to functionality and performance
Because using one technique is more cost-effective

parts.content: State two additive manufacturing techniques commonly used in 3D printing.
parts.markscheme:
- Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) <marks>A1</marks>
- Stereolithography (SLA) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each correctly identified technique, up to [2 max]. Accept other valid techniques such as Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) or Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM).</note>

options.content: change properties in response to stimuli

parts.content: Explain the difference between rapid prototyping and mass production.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that rapid prototyping is used for testing and validating early-stage models <marks>1</marks>

- **Outline** how it allows for quick design iterations and modifications before final manufacturing <marks>1</marks>

- **Describe** mass production as the efficient creation of large quantities of identical final products <marks>1</marks>

- **Explain** that mass production prioritizes scalability, consistency, and speed for high-volume output <marks>1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each valid distinction or explanation, up to [4 marks max].</note>

questions.specification: Which method uses CAD models to create quick physical prototypes?
Why is 5D printing significant?

options.content: It allows rotation along more axes

questions.specification: Which technique is used when joining dissimilar materials?

Which of the following is best represented by this image?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/81a031c6-7996-4d2f-a47f-388ca0a959dc.jpeg)

options.markscheme: 4D simulation refers to 3D CAD modelling with the addition of time-related data, often used in construction planning, which is not specifically depicted here.
Injection moulding is a physical manufacturing process used for mass production, not a digital design tool.
Lathe prototyping involves subtractive manufacturing on a physical machine, whereas the image shows a digital software interface.
The image shows a 3D model being created or manipulated within a software interface, which is the primary function of Computer-Aided Design (CAD).

questions.specification:
2. What feature distinguishes 4D printing from traditional 3D printing?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXd8L1bxRAEEKdaxXRGpuUaCUiI_5pRW4np8WSB2QjkiUx3bVfoCC-uHcRMoI9xl9ss_hN3uw2OT7r-vfrPxwY89sSBAwHQBNw_lwkdT_UdYMV6RmbAlbsKWssMUD-CLzam8yWAZ?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

parts.content: Evaluate how combining different techniques helps create a complex mechanical product (e.g. a drone).
parts.markscheme:
- Additive: 3D print lightweight frame parts. <marks>1 mark</marks>


- Subtractive: CNC-mill metal motor mounts. <marks>1 mark</marks>


- Joining: Use fasteners to assemble modular components. <marks>1 mark</marks>


- Finishing: Powder-coat for weather resistance. <marks>1 mark</marks>


- Each technique contributes to performance and weight optimization. <marks>1 mark</marks>


- Integration reduces production cost while maintaining quality. <marks>1 mark</marks>


- Enhances repairability and upgradability. <marks>1 mark</marks>


- Balances strength, precision, and aesthetics. <marks>1 mark</marks>


- Offers flexibility in prototyping and scaling. <marks>1 mark</marks>


*Award [1] for each clearly evaluated point or technique, up to [9 max].*

questions.specification: A company is developing a new line of electric kettles. The casing, internal metal heating plate, and control switch are all produced using different manufacturing techniques to balance cost, durability, and production speed.

parts.content:
The kettle’s casing is produced by heating a plastic sheet and forming it over a mould using vacuum suction.

![Kettle casing manufacturing process shown (Figure 1)](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c7ae0c49-ec66-4741-a2a1-67d46a9871f2)

i.) Identify the manufacturing technique shown in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme: - Thermoforming (vacuum forming). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: ii.) Explain one advantage and one limitation of this technique for producing kettle casings.
parts.markscheme:
- Advantage: Low tooling cost makes it ideal for small-scale production. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limitation: Surface detail and dimensional accuracy are limited compared to injection moulding. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The heating plate at the base of the kettle is made by removing material from a steel block using a programmed computer-controlled machine.

![Heating plate manufacturing process shown (Figure 2)](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d9addccd-5c43-43fa-99b1-b4e540b2b44c)

i.) Identify the manufacturing technique shown in **Figure 2** to produce the heating plate.
parts.markscheme: - CNC machining (subtractive process). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: ii.) Describe two reasons why this production method is appropriate for producing the heating plate.
parts.markscheme:
- Enables precise tolerances and surface flatness necessary for effective thermal contact or heat transfer. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allows consistent reproduction of complex internal grooves or details. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The kettle’s on/off switch is produced in large volumes using a highly automated process involving a steel mould.

![Injection moulding process shown (Figure 3)](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/daeb4e97-99d9-41ff-b3ff-f7641bf009c0)

Explain why injection moulding is suitable for producing the kettle switch.
parts.markscheme:
- Capable of producing large quantities with high accuracy and detail. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Once moulds are created, unit costs are low due to automation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate how combining these three different manufacturing techniques (thermoforming, CNC machining, injection moulding) contributes to cost-efficiency and product performance in the final kettle.
parts.markscheme:
- Thermoforming keeps costs low for casing, ideal for moderate production volumes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- CNC ensures precision and durability for the heating plate, enhancing safety and function. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Injection moulding provides consistency and low cost for switches, important for user interface. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Using different methods allows material optimisation based on functional needs. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- However, it may introduce complexity in production planning and quality control across techniques. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Overall, this hybrid approach improves value and manufacturability while balancing trade-offs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Which process builds up layers to form an object?
Topic 3: Modelling

parts.content: Describe the benefits of 4D printing in advanced product design.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe how 4D printed objects respond to environmental stimuli (e.g., temperature, pH), allowing for autonomous behavior without manual intervention. <marks>1</marks>

- Explain how the technology reduces the need for external actuation mechanisms, resulting in lighter products with fewer mechanical failure points. <marks>1</marks>

- Identify the benefit of high adaptability in specialized fields, such as medical implants that expand in the body or aerospace components that self-deploy. <marks>1</marks>

- Describe how the use of shape memory polymers enables objects to undergo dynamic transformations, which can improve efficiency in storage and transportation. <marks>1</marks>

4 marks total

*Award [1] for each clearly described benefit, up to [4 max].*

questions.specification: Subtractive manufacturing usually involves...

options.content: removing parts from a block or sheet

questions.specification:
What does this image illustrate in the context of product design?

What is the role of joining techniques?

options.content: To bond materials either temporarily or permanently

questions.specification: A product design team is manufacturing parts for a children's ride-on toy car. The hollow body shell, metal steering column, and internal gear lever are all made using different production techniques to suit their specific material and functional requirements.

parts.content:
The body shell of the ride-on car is created by rotating a heated mould containing polyethylene powder until the plastic melts and coats the interior surface.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/281f5747-7251-4c25-8448-b5bc959185f4)
i.) Identify the manufacturing technique used to produce the car’s body shell.
parts.markscheme:
Rotational moulding. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 mark] for correct identification.*

parts.content: ii.) Explain two advantages of this technique when used for producing children's toys.
parts.markscheme:
Produces seamless hollow parts that are strong and durable. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Moulds can be changed relatively easily to create variations in design. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 mark] per valid advantage up to [2 max].*

parts.content:
The steering column is a solid metal part made by pouring molten aluminium into a sand mould, which is broken away after cooling.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a2156191-0022-414a-ab78-57de0e5c8496)

i.) Identify the manufacturing process shown in Figure 2.
parts.markscheme:
Sand casting. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 mark] for correct identification.*

parts.content: ii.) Describe two disadvantages of the above method for producing precise components.
parts.markscheme:
Surface finish is rough and may require additional machining. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Lower dimensional accuracy compared to die casting or CNC machining. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 mark] per disadvantage up to [2 max].*

parts.content:
The gear lever assembly is produced in quantities of 10,000 per month using a semi-automated production line.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/fe9f0769-cf91-4083-8da3-ef0e5968e896)

Identify the most appropriate type of production for the gear lever shown in **Figure 3**.
parts.markscheme:
Batch production. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 mark] for correct identification.*

parts.content: Discuss the implications of using batch production for a toy manufacturer in terms of flexibility, cost, and lead time.
parts.markscheme:
Offers flexibility to produce different variations of the product. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Allows moderate unit costs due to some economies of scale. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Reduces inventory waste compared to mass production. <marks>1 mark</marks>

However, machine retooling between batches increases downtime. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Useful for seasonal demand or limited edition toys. <marks>1 mark</marks>

May not be cost-effective for extremely high-volume items. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 mark] per relevant discussion point up to [6 max].*

questions.specification: A company that produces components for household appliances selects different manufacturing techniques depending on the material, geometry, and production volume of each part.

parts.content:
The heat-resistant rubber gasket shown in Figure 1 is shaped using heat and pressure applied within a mould.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/9c66d23d-d859-44d5-8a07-0e477ebbe994)

Identify the manufacturing technique used to create the rubber gasket.
parts.markscheme: - Compression moulding. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State one reason why compression moulding is suitable for producing this gasket.
parts.markscheme: - Allows precise shaping of rubber parts that require durability and thermal resistance. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State two limitations of compression moulding for large-scale industrial use.
parts.markscheme:
- Longer cycle times compared to injection moulding. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Difficult to achieve complex geometries. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
A complex aluminium casing for an electric blender is produced using a subtractive manufacturing process guided by digital design.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/3da2fd15-d999-416b-8968-622048033f1d)

Identify the process used to manufacture the casing in Figure 2.
parts.markscheme: - CNC milling. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe two benefits of using the above method for producing precision components like this casing.
parts.markscheme:
- High dimensional accuracy and repeatability. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Automated toolpaths reduce human error and allow complex contours. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
A manufacturer uses a mould to form thousands of identical zinc alloy handles for kitchen appliances.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/9179e54e-c35d-4d5e-95b1-25a31ea3cb6f)

Compare the suitability of die casting and sand casting for manufacturing the handle shown in Figure 3, with reference to finish, cost, and speed.
parts.markscheme:
- Die casting offers smoother surface finishes requiring less post-processing. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It provides faster production cycles for high volumes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Tooling costs are higher in die casting, but unit cost is lower at scale. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sand casting has lower tooling costs but is slower and yields rougher finishes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Die casting is more suitable for producing durable and visually appealing consumer products. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sand casting may be better for low-volume or prototyping purposes. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A high-end kitchenware brand is developing a limited-edition chef’s knife that combines precision, durability, and aesthetic appeal. The knife consists of a 3D-printed ergonomic handle, a CNC-machined stainless steel blade, and a finished protective coating to improve longevity. Each knife is handcrafted in small batches for professional chefs.

parts.content:
A technician inspects a batch of 3D-printed knife handles with a honeycomb internal structure designed to reduce weight while maintaining strength.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f6d5a658-e2f2-4900-9e59-a85068ae37a5)

Identify the manufacturing category used to produce the handle shown in **Figure 1**, and state one benefit of this category.
parts.markscheme:
The handle in Figure 1 is made using additive manufacturing (3D printing). <marks>1 mark</marks>

This technique allows for complex internal geometries like honeycomb structures for weight reduction. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [2 max].*

parts.content:
A CNC milling machine removes material from a stainless steel block to form the sharp profile of a chef’s blade.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/96310b81-8563-475a-b35a-66702ed24c02)

Referring to **Figure 2**, describe two reasons why subtractive techniques are suitable for manufacturing the blade.
parts.markscheme:
CNC machining provides tight tolerances for sharpness and edge uniformity. <marks>1 mark</marks>

It allows for repeatable, high-precision shaping of hard materials like stainless steel. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 mark] per correct explanation, up to [2 max].*

parts.content: Suggest how joining techniques may be applied when combining the handle and blade, and justify the importance of precision in this step.
parts.markscheme:
Joining techniques such as mechanical fastening or adhesive bonding can be used to attach the handle and blade. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Precision is critical to ensure alignment for proper knife balance. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Misalignment could affect cutting performance or safety. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Ensures tight tolerances are maintained between distinct materials (metal and polymer). <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 mark] per well-explained point, up to [4 max].*

parts.content:
The knife blade undergoes anodizing and is coated with a matte-black ceramic finish for durability and a premium look.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/1507a9d3-398f-43cb-9eb6-2efcaf871ab9)

Explain how the coating and anodizing processes shown in **Figure 3** contribute to the knife’s quality.
parts.markscheme:
Anodizing increases the blade’s resistance to corrosion and wear. <marks>1 mark</marks>

The ceramic coating enhances surface hardness and durability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

The finish contributes to the knife’s aesthetic appeal, aligning with its premium branding. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 mark] per relevant explanation, up to [3 max].*

parts.content: Discuss how combining additive, subtractive, and finishing techniques benefits the production of this high-end knife, while considering potential trade-offs.
parts.markscheme:
Benefits:

Additive manufacturing enables custom ergonomic handle designs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Subtractive techniques ensure blade accuracy and quality edge geometry. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Finishing improves longevity and visual branding. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Trade-offs:

Combining methods may increase production complexity and time. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Requires coordination across multiple manufacturing setups, raising cost. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Differences in material properties (e.g., polymer and steel) may require special joining considerations. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 mark] per relevant benefit or limitation, up to [6 max].*

questions.specification: What does forming NOT involve?

parts.content: Compare subtractive and forming manufacturing techniques. Include examples.
parts.markscheme:
- Subtractive manufacturing involves the removal of material from a starting block to create a final shape. <marks>1</marks>
- Examples of subtractive techniques include milling, turning, or laser cutting. <marks>1</marks>
- Forming manufacturing involves changing the shape of a material through permanent deformation, without the removal or addition of material. <marks>1</marks>
- Examples of forming techniques include vacuum forming, forging, bending, or laminating. <marks>1</marks>
- Subtractive processes produce significant waste (scrap/swarf), whereas forming processes are near-net-shape and highly material-efficient. <marks>1</marks>
- Subtractive is often used for high-precision components and one-offs, while forming is typically suited for batch or mass production. <marks>1</marks>

*Award [1] for each well-explained comparison or example, up to [6].*

questions.specification: Which is a forming technique?

A manufacturer is producing a rechargeable torchlight. The torch’s housing is made from aluminium using CNC milling, and the lens casing is produced in batches using injection moulding. Precision is critical, especially in aligning the internal LED components, which requires tight tolerances and the use of automated jigs during assembly.

parts.content:
CNC milling a torchlight body from a solid aluminium block.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/146dd5f8-2893-4feb-89d0-ec48dfecc833)

i.)  Identify the manufacturing method shown in **Figure 1** and name one benefit of using this method for producing the torch’s body.
parts.markscheme:
The process in Figure 1 is subtractive manufacturing using CNC milling. <marks>1 mark</marks>

It allows for high precision and customisation in small or medium production runs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [2 max].*

parts.content: ii.) State two reasons why CNC machining often results in greater material wastage compared to moulding.
parts.markscheme:
CNC removes material from a solid block, leading to significant offcuts or waste. <marks>1 mark</marks>

It does not reuse excess material like injection moulding can. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 mark] per valid reason, up to [2 max].*

parts.content:
Injection moulding of the torch’s clear plastic lens casing for a batch production run.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c6401e06-ba54-4a4b-abfc-0dd31faf9944)

Explain why injection moulding, shown in **Figure 2**, is well-suited for batch production of the lens.
parts.markscheme:
Injection moulding can produce complex shapes quickly and repeatedly. <marks>1 mark</marks>

It is cost-effective after the initial tooling is done. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Suitable for medium-scale runs like batches due to low unit cost at scale. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 mark] per relevant point, up to [3 max].*

parts.content:
A jig is used in an automated assembly station to align and secure internal LED components.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c9c419e3-f59a-4011-a904-0f947f276270)

Using **Figure 3**, explain the function of jigs in ensuring accuracy during automated assembly of electronic components.
parts.markscheme:
Jigs hold components in precise positions during assembly. <marks>1 mark</marks>

In automated setups, they ensure repeatable accuracy across units. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Help maintain tight tolerances required for electronics. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Reduce human error and speed up assembly time. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 mark] per well-explained benefit, up to [4 max].*

parts.content:
The company wants to scale from batch to mass production while ensuring high quality and precision.

Discuss the advantages and limitations of automation and the use of jigs/fixtures in achieving this goal.
parts.markscheme:
Advantages:

Automation improves speed, consistency, and scalability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Jigs and fixtures reduce variability, supporting tight tolerances. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Decreases reliance on skilled labour. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Limitations:

High initial cost for machines and jigs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Limited flexibility once production is scaled. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Maintenance and calibration are required to sustain quality. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 mark] per well-developed point. Expect balanced evaluation up to [6 max].*

questions.specification: Which technique is associated with 3D printing for testing designs?

What best explains how a 5-axis printer differs from conventional 3-axis 3D printing?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/8b3ad5ef-4345-4797-b5bf-4ace73b32e3d.jpeg)

options.content: It uses metal filaments instead of polymer-based materials.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Both 3-axis and 5-axis printers can utilize a variety of materials, including metals and polymers. The difference lies in the movement of the machine, not the filament type.

options.content: It utilizes two additional rotational axes for the print head or build platform.
options.markscheme: Correct. While conventional 3D printers operate on three translational axes (X, Y, and Z), 5-axis printers add two rotational axes. This allows the printer to approach the part from different angles, often eliminating the need for support structures.

options.content: It relies on fused deposition modeling (FDM) technology to create parts.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. FDM is a common printing method used by both 3-axis and 5-axis machines; it does not define the difference between them.

options.content: It eliminates the need for computer-aided design (CAD) software.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. 5-axis printing still requires CAD models. In fact, it typically requires more complex CAD and CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacturing) software to generate the toolpaths for the extra axes.

questions.specification: Where is additive manufacturing most commonly applied?
